They have two private karaoke rooms, and the main bar available for rent at $75 an hour. For the main bar, you can generally just hang out and sing songs with random strangers for free if no other group has it rented. Also, If you spend a certain amount during your stay, I believe you get the room free of charge. I organize quite a few parties there, but I also go just to hang out. It's worth calling ahead to find out if the bar is open (not rented out) before you go.

Needless to say, Fuji is a blast when rented out for large parties. Get a group of 20-30 friends together for a birthday shindig and prepare for awesome. Ask Sam to teach you the secret Konami code you must input to search the Korean menus in the karaoke machine for your favorite song. Drink a lot of water, or your throat will undoubtedly end up like coarse grade sand paper with paperclips inexplicably attached to it. What were you thinking, ruining good sand paper like that?

The bar staff whips up some good drinks, and will even indulge you and your group in sake bombs if you feel the need to bastardize a rich ancient culture with your Western alcoholism. I suggest a tall, cool Sapphire Collins for the manly men. Sam also knows how to make this chocolate strawberry "martini" thing for the ladies. Also, try both the Pear and Watermelon Soju "martinis." Or, as I said before, just get a large pot of house sake and four or five 22 oz Sapporos, mix, and begin belting out "Pour Some Sugar on Me."

If you are for real about sake, they do have a respectable selection of cold sakes available, complete in a menu with little descriptions like "dry with floral notes and an understated zest" or something like that. I've tried a few, and since I'm not a sake connoisseur, I can only say that I prefer drier, less creamy (is that even a word I can use in this situation?) sakes. As an alcoholic, I can say that I will drink them all.

Oh, and try the green tea tempura ice cream. It's like all the other green tea tempura ice cream you've had before, which is to say good.

It was somewhat of a mystery to get to the elevator and I thought from the fancy nature of coat check that maybe coming from work i was under-dressed.
After the long elevator ride up to the 37th floor i was pelasantly surprised to see a very lux, yet intimate and trendy dining area. The kitchen was in plain view behind glass. Everything looks very clean and well lit back there. The chef's were hard at work.
Our view of Philadelphia was spectacular, unfortunately we were part of the scenic part of Philly but all the lights and highway traffic we could see from our seat made it just as entertaining.


Service was timely and very friendly. Everyone was very professional and pelasant. Our waiter could have smiled a bit more but the hostess was delightful.
The food was dainty (small) but very tastey. I was actually surprised by the prices. They were not as expensive as I thought they might be based on the decor. My romaine salad was $6, soup was also about $6. A glass of wine was about $8 and crab cake appetizers were about $12. NOT BAD! the risotto balls were a little on the bland and salty side but the salad and soup were delicious. the crab cakes were also very fresh and seasoned very well.
some main dish prices i noticed were salmon = $22, chicken = $23. they were all about that which still is not expensive, say compared to buddhakan. 

we didnt have time for desert but the chef sent us some gingered grapefruit and a chocolate truffle. both were a sweet satisfying end to our meal. 

i will definitely be back. this is a great place for a girls night or a romantic weeknight date.

So this nail spot is around the corner from a bagel shop small but clean.  I just got a regular manicure which was much needed.  Simple manicure, but when I went to dry my nails the dryer they used did not have the UV light which is important for baking the polish on.  The cost was also 15 dollars yes, thats right 10 + 5.  So while I was drying my nails my manicurist began to give me a massage.  I mean it was sooooooo needed and she really got into a knot I had in my back.  So I will go again because the massage was great and worth the cost of the manicure.

I was in Tampa for a job interview and I stopped by International Plaza because I heard Ruehl was going out of business and everything was 50% off. I guess International Plaza is the ritzy mall of Tampa. It has big department stores, Zara, my favorite J. Crew, and some more affordable ready-wear brands found in your standard mall. I was sad they did not have Anthropologie, which comes as a big surprise to me considering they had a motley of other specialty stores, including Loccitane. 

I think it's a nice mall with a not-bad layout. I think it's worth a visit if you're visiting the area.
Just ok.  Low Mein tasted burnt.  However, the other dishes are probably good because everyone in their was from the neighborhood.

Dinner Tues 2/2 not much of an appetizer selection  so we started with the soup, it was good, just soup. Hubby ordered the steak and fries ($21) ordered medium rare came out more toward medium but it's a thin cut of steak so not surprised. It was very good and had a nice smokey taste to it. I had the ahi tuna with ginger glaze, potatos and slaw ($26) was good, nothing great, for that price I expected it to be really good. The salad dressings were very good and you could tell they were homemade. The service was very good as well. The drinks perfectly poured. All in all a good night, but I'm not in a hurry to return, their menu just didn't impress me that much. A lot of items you could make or get everyday anywhere just with better service and a nice atmosphere. Definitely worth at least one try though.
Easy, yummy food, service may not be the best, but they are friendly! Great place for a cheap, family friendly night out.

Loved the BBQ Sandwich Sliders.
Loved the Mac N' Cheese.
Loved the chalkboard wall displays.
Loved the atmosphere

Liked the french fries. 
Liked the wait staff.

Disliked the wait to be seated.

Good enough for me! I'd go back. Maybe next time i'll try the turducken burger - sounds like it could be quite appetizing.

Devon is one of those places I think of for "special occasions."  Like Tuesday.  Or St. Swithun's Day. 

I took a date there the first time I went two summers ago, and later found myself whispering "thank you, fabulous new restaraunt team, for making the rest of this evening possible!"

I've been back many, many times since (the attendant in the parking garage around the corner now recognizes me).  

That being said, a couple pointers:
- I think sitting in the lounge area upstairs (particularly when the doors are open to the street and you can people-watch the comings and goings on Rittenhouse Square) is preferable to the main room downstairs.

- If you aren't happy, speak up!  The staff seems to really want your meal to be perfect.  For example, I generally love the seafood bisque, but it was lukewarm on one occasion.  Shame on me for not saying anything to the server, who was horrified when I mentioned it at the end of the meal.  (I had an indifferent server once and she was gone a week later when I returned. Good for you, Devon!)

- I think I've had meals that were marginally better at The Capital Grille, but I love that while you can find Devon in a few other cities, it seems more unique.

- The Opah is to die for.

My number-one favorite Philly restaurant.  I can't think of another place in the city with more intimate, cozy atmosphere, from the cobblestone street to the historic stone and brick and wood structure.

The food has always been a solid 8 or 9, whether I have been there with just one other person or on the two occasions I have entertained parties of eight or ten.  (Unlike most restaurants that put big parties at a huge bare table in the back, we were fit into a cozy alcove where we didn't disturb the other diners, but still felt like we were "in" the same room as everyone else.)

I have occasionally wished that the wait-staff were more enthusiastic or engaging, but they have never lacked for competence.

I must confess that the Mystery Dinner Theater almost scared me off the first time (I imagined something out of a bad Pocono dinner-theater).  I can't say what the show might be like -- I've never been -- but everything I have seen makes Bistro Romano a solid choice, particularly for a romantic dinner for two.

Pointer:  the private table in the wine celler sounded like a great idea and a very exclusive place to be, but the one time I opted for it I just a little isolated:  there is more than enough intimacy in the booths already).

"Tourist trap."

I went with a friend on the spur of the moment a few weeks ago.  We thought we'd drop in to see a mutual acquaintance who worked there.  Bad idea.  It was a Saturday night and there was a junior-high-aged cheerleading competition of some kind in town:  the Hard Rock was jammed with pony-tails, glitter, and terrycloth shorts with "CHEER" emblazoned across the ass.

People who couldn't get a table just took their party of fourteen into the bar and took over every table while their kids ran back and forth.  After enduring it for about 30 minutes I tried to stab myself to death with a butter knife but was stopped by a hefty real estate agent from Minneapolis who was in town with her nieces.

If the staff had done something -- anything -- to maintain order (like keeping the little treasures out of the bar area) it would have been ok, but this was an "N-I-G-H-T-M-A-R-E . . . nightmare! Go! Go! Go!"

Another place I live dangerously close to...

 Now I understand that their prices are a tad bit higher than your  average pizzeria, but this isn't your run of the mill joint. Rustica buys top of the line gourmet ingredients to put the love into their pizzas and sandwiches. Their creativity seen in each pie is worth every penny. 

My favorite pizza is the tartufo... steak, cheese, onions, and truffle cream sauce. Get ready to smell!!!! mmmmm.. (the tartufo cheesesteak is the same winning item stuffed in a roll.)

Another item I'm a sucker for here are their french fries. I don't eat french fries very often, but when I do - I want them from this place. They are the perfect thin, crunchy fry, similar to the ones found at McDonalds - minus the lack of breakdown (reference: Supersize Me).

Pick up a beer or two at the Foodery and you've got the perfect meal.

Obviously I'm a fan, the bartender AND the bouncer recognize us.  But lately I haven't been impressed with the crowd that I've seen gathering at here.  Last night, granted it was a Thursday, the bar had an unfavorable ratio(21:7, guys:girls).  Maybe it's the extensive beer selection, maybe there's something else going on.  All I know is I'm probably going to be on a Local 44 hiatus for a few months.

I had the Ruspante pizza to go so did not experience dining in.  Very average pizza.  My crust was not cooked enough.  I usually have Bazils to Go delivered and I'm sticking with Bazils.  Really was hoping for more pasta items on the menu (only two) so I would have something else to try

It's nice to have something new in town, especially in the Downtown Tampa area, to check out, but the Museum of Art is a bit of a disappointment. It isn't very big, we probably thoroughly looked through most of the exhibits in just twenty minutes. My favorite part of the museum was probably the Greek and Roman Antiquities, the paintings weren't too appealing to me and the photography there wasn't captivating. The building itself is nice. The surrounding area is great to just enjoy on a nice sunny day including the dog park nearby and the awesome view of the University of Tampa. We were able to go on a nice student discount so our ticket was only five dollars. I would say it was worth that, but probably wouldn't go for a higher price.

Not only does their sesame bread pair amazingly well with *anything*, it is delicious by itself!

There have been many an occasion when my mamma and I have just eaten it fresh from the bakery, passing the baguette between ourselves on our walk home.

A must-try before you die!

The Good:  Great cuts of cow- Filet and ribeye cooked as ordered  each time.  King crab gnocchi and lobster mac and cheese- rich and delish but sits in your stomach like a brick (worth it!),  shanghai calamari- light and crisp w/ some bite. The architecture- this place is gorgeous, it rivals Union Trust. A huge wine list.  According to Law Abiding Citizen you can kill a man w/ their porterhouse's bone.

The OK: Salmon- a bit overcook for me -serves me right for getting fish at a steakhouse. The dessert-I had the bread pudding and some chocolate thing - don't remember much about either.

The Bad: This biatch is expensive. No Franzia on the wine list.

The Ugly:  A lot of older women who r losing the battle against gravity dressed like snookie and jwow. Cover yourselves up grandhookers people r eating here.

Hangover Killah !!!

I worked with a Viatnamese friend who introduced me to Pho soup. He would order for us and minutes later a boiling, yes boiling bowl of soup would come where we would cook our meat and vegetables.

Their coffee is pretty good too =)

Went with the RW Menu

If you are trying to cut down on your sodium level, do not go here. 8oz Filet was WAY too salty. What also ruined my dining experience would be the wait to be seated. I felt like i was standing around for over an hour even w/ reservation. I would have to say that that is a bit ridiculous. 

What saved this location from a lower rating would be their dessert. The chocolate mousse and bread pudding were quite fabulous.
Shape up guys!

Firstly  you have to go into the brand new restaurant, Sampan, you will notice the beautiful wall lightings! the bar is in the center of the room. and all the hot new sexy chef and server in  the open kitchen...
The food was amazing! It's not easy to find satisfying Asian restaurant for me ( who is from Asia) in Philadelphia usually. beef satay was great.  the lamb chops need i say more ... and a very intriguing sashimi with a vinagrette and bacon cannot go wrong with that ...
The Lobster Dumpling was soooo good. All the flavor was in the dumpling, so you do not need to put any sauce. 
The soft serve ice cream was cute and tasty as a dessert. It comes in 3 tiny cones on little silver tray  we got to mix our flavors the server idea and its was worth it ..... ..

From the outside, the building and grounds are very impressive. The park is very nice and a nice place to walk around or sit and have coffee. The museum is something of a disappointment considering the size of the building. More thought could have gone into layout and display.

The Ancient Grecian is exhibit is crammed into the smallest available room. It's also tough to figure out which item in the case goes with which descriptive card. In one case, they had mixed pieces from the 4th Century AD with items from 400 BC.

You have to be a serious fan of Matisse since the Matisse exhibit occupies nearly half the available space. 

The Sonos Cafe in the Museum needs to figure out that a panini is made with Italian bread rather than French bread. 

Hopefully the museum will iron out some of these issues and I'll be able to give it a better rating in the future.

I was so impressed with their food from few days ago, I went back again to try others!
Wild Mashroom salad with Goat Cheese, Puffed Rice, Truffles was wonderful mixture of taste. It felt familier taste but yet very unique and delicious! Even my husnad who doesn't usually eat Vegitable, was gabbling up. haha
I also tried " Pork Bahn Mi" since all other people who on yelp looks like they liked it. The wrapping was just brown paper wrap with paint tape, but I guess that's the artsy part of this restaurant. You know I don't have to explain how was the taste! G R E A T
Chiken wing was like the one I had once in Japan. Nagoya style, which you don't see much even in NY.  ( It's thai style also?)
oops, I almost forgot to tell you about the drink! 
Scorpion Punch ( looks like sangria with fruit) was so strong like Long Island Ice Tea. If you want to get drunk, that's the one to have! Recommend sharing with your love ones. I heard it's usually $24, but 50% off because of the snow. We felt lucky! I 
 
Next time, I want to try the Ramen Noodles. I haven't found my favorite noodle place in Phili yet. 

Looking forward to see you Sampan!

With an exterior this bright Desi Chaat beckons to be yelped about, so here it is:
Well I made a new friend tonight.  Hassan, the owner told me all about the uniquely Indian street snack called chaat.  He was more than willing to satisfy my every curiosity when it came to my newly discovered dish and even let me taste some of the ingredients he was using.  Served as meal in this country instead of just a snack, chaat is the salty/sweet/spicy/chrunchy Desi Chaat specializes in.  As mainly a takeout place, Desi Chaat is very clean and has only a few seats at a counter.  The menu is very large and everything seems very fresh.  At $6.75 I'm happy to report that the chicken samosa filled me right up to the top.  Pictures above!!

I come here mainly for their grab n go summer rolls and whatever else I feel like snatching away from the deli counter. I love it so. The rolls are stuffed to the max with fresh goodness and inexpensive.  
I'll also eat here late-night if I'm in the mood or if Pho 75 is closed.  They make a great Pho, and their takeaway style works great so I can have everything separated and ready to assemble when i get home without the noodles going mushy or beef getting hard.

Best pizza I've ever had, and to my surprise, BEST gazpacho ever. Their sauce is exactly what I like, smooth texture and plenty of garlic. The 1/4 home baked cookies are great, as well.

When I lived in Tucson I hit Rocco's maybe 25 times, and every rare visit I try and swing by.

From a Vegan perspective: excellent non-greasy falafel and fresh vegetable ingredients.  Fairly authentic Middle-Eastern taste in hummus, etc. (and my mom is Lebanese).  Just have to kick a few self-absorbed students out of your way.
Service could be a little more engaged.

Stop by for a low-key dinner. Prepare to bump elbows with other patrons if you stop by here during prime meal hours and don't be shocked by the *quaint feel of the restaurant. Bring a bottle of wine or other booze of choice and order the Chicken Corn/Egg Soup, Salt Baked Seafood, Sizzling Beef Pepper Steak and Veggie of the day (usually they're similar to spinach) fried with Garlic. Sharing is caring so eat family style. They'll give you a big bowl of white rice and some water glasses for your wine. They're classy, what can I say? Food service is a bit slow and feels even slower if you're hungry. Other than that the service is good and the check will make your wallet as happy as your tummy.
*And by quaint, I mean dirty.
So good!  Great value, the food is awesome, i eat here several times a month, and have never been disappointed!  Go!  Get the sicilian salad with sauteed chicken!

To those of us who have been coming to Guero for years, I can say this: it's over. Seriously, just go down the street to BK's, or to one of the possibly hundreds of little carts selling Sonoran dogs around town. It's just not worth it anymore. Guero, this one especially, has been ruined.
I had never been to this Oracle location before this weekend, although I had had several very positive experiences at their original, 12th Ave location. One of my fonder Tucson memories is sitting under the tin roof at Guero's patio on a quiet (read: "no crowd") summer evening while the sun set and a monsoon storm raged in front of us. But that's gone.
Saturday night, around dinner, I anticipate a wait, but a half hour for two dogs and a taco? Really? Another guy waiting for a pick-up order struck up a brief conversation with me, and seemed equally frustrated by the crowds. I think he ended up waiting longer than I did.
Next morning, I went out with friends for a breakfast. The place we wanted to patronize was closed (I guess they close every Valentines) and, not wanting to drive too far, we opted for Guero on Oracle, as I was surprised to see that they had a breakfast available when I was there the night before.
As soon as I saw the parking lot, I knew that this was a bad decision. It was still packed. Inside, there was a line to order, but nothing too bad. We pondered over the menu and I noticed, on the breakfast burritos (the majority of their menu), that most only had two ingredients mentioned and a sign above that said "Additional Ingredients: $0.50." Sure enough, yeah, they charge you for what most places give you for free. The chorrizo and egg burrito is just chorrizo + egg. No potatoes, no cheese. That's extra. And, at $3.50, you're already paying over $1 more than you would at other places. I opted for the chorrizo + papas and added cheese. My companions also ordered a variation on a chorrizo burro.
I think it was about 30 minutes later that our number got called. That was a long, long wait too because, by then, all of us were very hungry. What came out was just awful. My burrito, which was supposed to have potatoes, had these little slivers of fried potato on the top that you only knew were there by their crunch. The cheese was pretty much non-existent also. What there was lots of was grease, and that eventually escaped via a hole at the bottom of mine. But all of us kind of agreed: chorrizo, not so much. Seriously, if I'm paying more than most places for a chorrizo burro, I would expect it to actually taste better than other places. Not the case at all. And, with all of that grease, I needed a soda when I got home (they're really overpriced there, at Guero) to just wash the taste out of my mouth.
The previous day, I had had a breakfast burrito at Viva, one of my old standards. Bacon + papas (real papas) + cheese + egg: $2.80 w/ tax, and that included free coffee (not the best, but coffee nonetheless). WTF is going on? I don't know. Even Nico's, which is always more expensive, has that same burro for a little over $3. And, yeah, Nico's is way better than this. Even Viva's better.
Now, I'll give it to Guero, based on my other Sonoran dog experiences at other places, they have the best. The sauteed onions, the bun and the sauces are all just better here. Even the rivals down the street don't have as good of a dog, and I betcha they know it too (although they have better carne). But, holy shit, it is not worth this sort of trouble. The media attention has ruined this place, and I'm not going back again. I'll take a slight hit and just eat the thing at one of the carts or at BK's itself. I mean, they're still good, but they're just no Guero. Oh well.

Second visit to Blanco was last night, for Valentine's. They had a fantastic special: one appetizer, two entrees, one dessert and two drinks (margarita or select wine), $35. Yeah...
The previous experience had just been for wine and a little munch food. The view that they have up there is just worth going there for, even if the food wasn't great. Aside from the stupidly fancy places, like next door's Anthony's, the view doesn't get much better in town. But the food is actually very, very good.
First, this is not Mexican. I mean, it's f'in La Encantada. You shouldn't expect su abuelita's cooking up there. If you come looking for the sort of Mexican that you get all over the basin, then you're going to be disappointed. This is the sort of fancy, nouveau, chic-ness that's, sometimes unfortunately, made its way down from Phoenix in recent years. But these guys do it well.
For the appetizer we opted for the nachos. Let me say first that I LOVE THEIR CHIPS. Wow. Maybe it's that they start with some really tortillas in the first place, but they do make their own chips here and they're very, very good. The salsa is also very tasty, with enough cilantro to make it enjoyable, but nothing too extreme, although not spicy enough for me (or even my relatively spicy-unfriendly girlfriend). The nachos had a lot of stuff on them, mainly a lot of their queso blanco, which is good. I wouldn't just order these, I think but, then again, the rest of the meal was so good that these were very much overshadowed.
For drinks, we opted the margaritas: I had the regular, she had the pomegranate. We had both had these things before on our last visit and loved them. They seem to pride themselves on their 'ritas: no mix, fresh lime (you can taste it), good tequila and not too strong. My only gripe was that, I asked for low salt and got a glass with half salt. Still a fantastic drink though.
Entrees came out pretty quickly. Maybe a little too quickly, since we weren't quite done with the appetizer. I had the "mole chicken" and she had the pescado which, on this night, was a sea bass (I guess they change it out regularly, so they don't note the fish on the menu). My mole chicken was more what I would call a "Southwestern chicken primavera rice bowl." The sauce on the chicken was brown and a little spicy, but it was not mole. I was happy with it though. The veggies (calabacitas, but also some tomatoes and other veggies) were grilled, and went well with the chicken and rice. The bowl was in layers, and I didn't even realize that there was rice until I stuck my fork in a little more. Kind of a nice surprise.
I mentioned the "not mole" thing to the waitress and I guess most people say that. That actually made me feel a bit better about the place, that there are really people coming there who know what mole should taste like. Like I said though, I enjoyed my dish and have no complaints about it, except that the "mole" is a bit misleading.
But I like this place. Everyone's nice and it's got a hip, although a bit noisy of an atmosphere. The music is mid- to late-90's pop rock, including some stuff that I despise, but I guess it beats muzak. Being Valentine's, there were lots of couples coming in, but there were also the noisy, sad, large groups of young single girls, who go out on Valentines and give each other gifts, pretending that they don't feel alone and unloved (note to sleazy guys: you missed your chance last night). They were there, and were a little annoying, just because of the noise.
Dessert came though and, wow, that's something that you should give a shot. We chose the chocolate cake over everything else, and I don't know that I've had better chocolate cake than that. First off, it's more or a mousse interior with a cake crust. It's served with ice cream, a dash of cinnamon (somewhere) and is not too big nor is it too sweet. Really a perfect little dessert there. I think it ran $5 normally, and I would have paid that in a heartbeat, considering the absolute garbage frozen desserts you get at most places for more money.
We got coffee too, just because that seemed nice on the relatively chilly night overlooking the city lights and the fading silhouettes of the western and southern mountains, and it was actually really good coffee. I'm a coffee snob, and I was totally happy with this stuff. Kind of a nice surprise because, I mean, they could have passed off some Taster's Choice at $2.50 a cup and they would have been on par with other places in town, but they actually put forth some good stuff.
And that's what makes these Fox restaurants so good: attention to detail and quality. He does a good job at the majority of his restaurants. Most of the time, you can get better food elsewhere but, here, for what it is, I don't know that you can do much better. If I wanted some flautas, a burrito or even a tamale, I have my choices and, yeah, they're better than what you'd get here, almost certainly. But I'll be back.

This is a Great restaurant, we went with friends on a whim and got there during the happy hour on Friday (which is actually during a reasonable time!) There was a lot of people but our waitress was on the spot and ready and served us really promptly. She came back often and took good care of us overall. We got our dishes which were real portions! I got the cimichanga with green chili in and out! The salsa was great as well as the beans and rice on the side! We got drinks! They were big and cheap! 2.50 for a giant mug of XX and 3 dollar giant margaritas that weren't watered down! The tortillas are the really tasty fatty kind that don't break if you try and fold them and the food wasn't too greasy. It was a little pricey but worth all of the money! Great place to go for atmosphere and food!

R2L has a great view.  It's decor is pretty cliche' cocktail lounge chic'.  The kitchen is glass walled and fun to see into.

OK that's where the charm ends though.

I met up with some friends there for cocktails hoping to find a classy happy hour and instead found a classy but rather underwhelming yet pricey short stay.  

We had a couple drinks each, none of which stood out as memorable.  Tried a few small dishes to share and they were good but not great.  Perhaps it's unfair to expect great but the prices are great, as in scale.

The staff didnt seem that knowledgeable but our server was friendly.

Nothing made me want to come back.  Go for the view maybe, and order one of the $5 beers, as they turned out better than my $12 cocktail.

Overall a 4.5, but curving to five because of the incredible service and extensive chaats that I can't wait to eventually sink my teeth into! With plenty of fruit and veggie chaats to choose from, veggie and chicken samosas, way too many wraps to chow down on, and Indian desserts to choose from; Desi Chaat house definitely gives you plenty of good reasons to go from "Mild to Wild!".

Upon first entering I was sold on just walking out with one of their lamb wraps, mango lassi, and a container of gulab jaman. But the person helping me behind the counter (I think owner) was amazing enough to walk me through the many varieties of chaat, and its plentiful varieties of ingredients. He was even friendly enough to scoop me up a complimentary small portion of fruit chaat for the road. extensive and helpful service like that just has to win you over.

My take out experience wasn't without it's foibles since the lid of my container of lassi managed to become loose and leave me with a fruity surprise. but it could be just as easily on the part of my error in carrying, and what I was able to salvage turned out to be super sweet. the gulab jaman wasn't as good as what I've had at New Delhi, but it managed to hit the spot all the same. While the particular chaat offered enough to pique my curiosity, it didn't fully deliver, and I found it to be way too sweet. But it made for a good preview that was enough to leave me interested in trying more.. The main event was the lamb  wrap and it was amazing. it had enough spices and sauces to rival the best of them at places like Saad's, and clocked at close to $1 less. So overall Desi Chaat House left me with plenty to enjoy, and I'm hooked on finding out more!

I would have rated them a two for what I ordered, but am upping them one cause my friend had the pho and said it was good..

so wut i had.. 
i ordered an "egg noodle" shrimp and pork hu tieu.. 
i got a "rice noodle" shrimp and pork hu tieu..
(we waited for awhile, so i didn't want them to have to correct their mistake and wait longer)
it sucked... doesn't even taste like hu tieu. 
it was bland..very very bland
i mean if they called it some other thing besides hu tieu it would have been okay.. 
like just some regular noodle soup..but they called it hu tieu..and I was expecting some hu tieu flavoring. 
and i still want my egg noddles... =(

i'll probably visit again.. but i'm not having any hu tieu here.

Passero's reopened a month or so ago... Being that I usually ride my bike to work entirely through winter I would have never noticed had the snow not nixed that.  Now every morning after getting off the EL and walking to Comcast Center I am totally tempted as I walk past them.  I can usually resist 3 out of 5 of my work days...but I let myself give in on two.  They have great coffee and ever changing roasts available.  I believe you have 4 different choices of coffee that change daily.  I always go with the organic choice they offer daily because it always sounds the most appealing.  Today I'm drinking the Guatemalan blend... so good.  I haven't tried any lattes yet but I'm going to add it to my list and splurge one day soon.  

I also can't forget to mention that everyone that works there is extremely friendly and chipper.  The service is stellar and quick, I don't understand why anyone would wait in line and the many Dunkin Donuts when they can just come here for a better product served by people that enjoy what they're doing.  They also sell soups at lunch that I haven't tried but bet are delicious.  Usually I'm kind of creeped out by the food places in suburban station, you know because its so dirty down there...but I love Passero's...its become my new coffee place for the time being.

Our Valentine's Day dinner was a nightmare.  We started out getting dirty and/or cracked wine glasses, we waited what seemed like forever for our food, they came back to our table twice to tell us something we had ordered they were now out of and then at the end they ran out of the dessert two of the group had selected.  

So why the 4 stars?  They couldn't possibly have been nicer about it with repeated apologies from our server and the manager, they gave us several glasses of wine to help compensate and they comped dessert for all 5 of us when originally only two were planning to have dessert.  That and the food that we did finally get was pretty darn terrific as it has been every meal we have ever had here.

It was a busy crazy night so I'll give them a pass this time.  Lubrication always helps.

Plus: One of the few places in the city to offer karaoke rooms!
Minus: Doing karaoke isn't as fun when you know the people in the room, and the graphics on the tv on STRANGE!!! But I digress.
Double minus: I threw my fedora into the crowd and knocked a SUPER expensive drink over...and graciously bought said person another drink.

I don't eat sushi, so I can't comment on that, but if you're looking to rent a karaoke room for a night, definitely check this spot out.

Woo hoo! A pho joint in my neighborhood! The place is new, decorated with dark wood tables, chairs, and flooring. The staff are friendly and welcoming. I have eaten here twice now and the pho is great, nice rich broth which is not too salty. This time I also sampled some of their other dishes. Their pork spring rolls are ok but not special. They do serve 2 kinds of banh mi, pork and chicken. We tried the chicken this time and it was amazing. The roll was nice and crisp and the meat well marinaded. I would have liked some more pickled veggies in it but I would eat those by the handful if you let me. In short I am excited to have a pho place so close and I am sure it will find a spot  in my regular rotation of restaurants.

*Note - they have now put some tables outside!

I think the food here is delicious.  Although service has definitely slowed since they moved I still appreciate the effort.  Try their huarache dish it is soooo good and the few margaritas I've tried so far are also very yum!  I wish them continued success!

Delicious Fettuccine Alfredo and Calamari cooked quickly. The eating time was actually less than the wait, and this one gets so packed compared to the Brandon Mall. The waiter was really friendly and quick to bring us bread and refill our drinks.

So I had to return to this over rated place due to a business associates staying nearby. Oh where to start. I guess I should be uber impressed I'm sitting by some football player everyone knows (except me who couldnt' care less) and eating a $15 shrimp cocktail, followed by a $32 piece of tuna, but I'm not. Nor was I impressed by a bathroom attendant at 7:00 on a Tuesday hovering around for tips. The $14 margarita was good, but was it worth $14??? NO. My associate ordered the $40 rack of lamb ordered medium that came out still bleeding to death. When she brought it to the servers attention he had the nerve to say "well really that's how it should be served" I thought she was going to blow a gasket! Of course the steaks are al a carte so she had the $8 baked potato on the side, rolled in so much salt you couldn't even cut into it without covering the inside in salt. So $250 I got a mediocre meal, an overpriced drink, a fairly well disappointed associate and the floor show of ass kissing the unknown football player to my right. You can guess if I'll return!

I used to love the place when it was a Vietnamese restaurant, but now it's a really popular Chinese one. I found out the place changed owners in the most embarrassing way. I came in during the day and didn't even look at the menu because I wanted to order "pho," which is a Vietnamese noodle soup. When I asked the server, he told me that they didn't carry it because they are a Chinese restaurant...I finally bothered to look at the cover of the menu...and there it was. 

The place changed a lot. The tables are spread out and have both small and large tables with the glass rotating circle to share all the food on. The atmosphere is really casual and the decorations are a bit tacky. But it's fun to have so many things to look at while waiting for the food. 

The service is depends on the day. Off days they are quick and good. Busy days like Wed. - Sun. it gets pretty packed during the evening. They sometimes take reservations if it's on that day. But normally there would be a line out the door. And the parking lot gets pretty crowded, so you might have to park at a neighboring plaza.  

Overall, the prices are reasonable for the amount and quality of the food you get. The food is pretty authentic and comes out hot! I go there more often than most Chinese restaurants. I pretty much go back for their tasty Salt & Pepper Calamari. It's the best because I like it really spicy! Although, recently I guess after some people complained, it's not as spicy. So for those spicy lovers out there, order it extra spicy. Also, their tofu dishes are awesome, and dare to compare their Sesame Chicken and Mongolian Beef to elsewhere. And on a cold evening, order their Crab Egg Drop soup, it's so hardy and good! Indeed a yummy place...

While some people complain that having a 7 course set menu is limiting, it is part of the experience at Marrakesh.  From the eggplant starter to the baklava dessert, the food was as flavorful as one would find in a Moroccan home.  If you want faster service on the weekend, make a later reservation (ie past 8pm).

This place is all about butter, and not in a good way.  Went for dinner with my SO's folks.  Chose it specifically because it's less noisy than Parc (and any number of other good places around) and because we assumed they could accommodate some basic  special requests.  Not really, though.  Service in general was really subpar.  Our server had no idea about the specials, including the oysters listed on the board by the entrance.  

I ordered a 1.25 lbs lobster, which was supposed to come out shelled, but in fact I still had to gnaw on it, to the get the meat out.  The whipped potatoes were really greasy and mushy.  My SO's father asked for a side that was simple and without too much butter, cream, etc.  They brought out risotto, which was chock full of buttery-creamy goodness.  The server said they do not have any vegetables that are not bathed in butter. 

The sweet butter biscuits , accompanied by copious amounts of butter, they serve instead of bread are an odd choice.  If I want dessert, I will ask for it.  Which we did and was the absolute worst.  I think it was something a chocolate something or other.  The top shell was so hard we had to use a knife to cut through.  We barely touched it.  When I asked for a cup of herbal tea, I was offered green tea.  I asked further and was then offered "raspberry" tea, which turned out to be a pretty decent tropical tea.  My SO had coffee and asked for skim milk, which they also did not have.  Strange, all around.

Strangely enough, I saw an episode of A&E's "Intervention" where a sort of sad and lonely guy came here to meet "friends" and no one showed up. So why not come here?
We headed out to South Tucson tonight with the idea of finally trying Micha's, which I had wanted to try for a while. Problem is that Micha's is closed on Mondays. Oh well. Plenty of other places in South Tucson to try: why not Rigo's?
Now, Rigo's is caddy-corner to one of my favorite places in the city, Crossroads Cafe, which is excessively cheap, has fantastic food and has one of the best chimichangas I've experienced (yeah, it was an experience). So I have to compare Rigo's to them, if only for the location.
We came in and were quickly seated in a restaurant that was maybe 35% full. They had an awkward guy playing awkward keyboard music in the corner, who would stop every once in a while and make it even more awkward because no one would clap.
Chips and salsa were brought out immediately, with two varieties: spicy and mild. The mild was full-on red, but the spicy had chunks of tomato, cilantro and onion in it. Spicy it was not, but the cilantro was nice. The mild was more like a fragrant ketchup. In all honesty, the taste was nice, it was just so non-spicy that I had a hard time with both of them.
I ordered the "Muy Very Good," a carne asade chimi slopped in enchilada sauce, and served with lettuce and pico. My companion got a red chile burro with rice and beans.
The food came out excessively fast. I mean, it was that sort of fast that makes you think that they already had it prepared and just slopped some of the sides and sauces on it.
When it came to the red chile burro, it tasted like that too. The tortilla was really dry, the red chile sauce had no spice to it and very little flavor. The beef strangely got a little better as you moved into the center of the burrito, which made me think that it really had been sitting under a heat lamp for a while.
I also have some experience with red chile burros at other places around town. I would say that, the majority of the time, Nico's is better, but I've had some at Viva that weren't as good. Although it completely depends on the Viva location. Now, of course, I'm comparing this "restaurant" burro with that of fast food but, if you justifiably compare to the red chile burros at some other actual restaurants in town that have it (Mosaic, Sanchez, El Charro, etc.), there's just no contest.
My chimi was not awful, but, if I was somewhere that I knew had better food, and got this, I wouldn't get it again. The enchilada sauce slopped on there was definitely homemade (it didn't taste like that stuff you get out of the can) but there was nothing special about it: it didn't have any subtle flavor, no spice, nothing interesting. The chimi itself was that same sort of low-grade or old tortilla that was in my companion's burro, but the fried-ness sort of covered it up a bit more. The carne asada inside tasted more like fajita meat, in that it was grilled on a skillet instead of "asada" as the name implies. Honestly, I've had better carne at Los Betos.
Maybe I've just gotten spoiled on Mexican food while living here, because I don't understand why you would eat here when there are so many better places in town. Like I said, try Crossroads, across the street.
Prices: a little on the high side for Mexican. My dish was $9 and my companion's was $7.50. For what it was (not even considering that it kind of sucked), it wasn't worth it. Across the street, at Crossroads, the chimi I got runs for about $6. Although it doesn't come with that kick-ass shredded iceberg lettuce on the side.
Service: not great. The waiter was nice, but he was a little slow and unattentive. He told us we could pay the check at the table but, after about five minutes of sitting there with the card on the table, we just took it up to the counter. I also asked for more chips at one point, he nodded in understanding, but they never came although, strangely, another bowl of the "spicy" salsa did.
So why 2*? Well, the atmosphere was actually pretty nice, and the food wasn't necessarily bad. I've been home for an hour or so, and haven't gotten sick, which is definitely a good sign. I mean, I think if you're looking for a place to take, say, your 70-year-old grandmother from Michigan who thinks of "Mexican food" as On the Border or some other complete, disgusting garbage, then go for it. Because, then, yeah, it'll be an improvement. But go to other places on the south side, or even more in the city (hell, El Charro even beats this place, hands down) and it's just way, way better. There's just no reason for this.

Outstanding pizza...but soooo much more, and all delectable!

Just spent a month in Tucson. Had been to Brazil's; wanted different Italian experience. Based on yelp, I tried Vera Amore...and ended up going back seven or eight times having something different usually, all of it great

The key thing you need to know is that their pizza is Neapolitan...explained on their website. The owner/chef went to Naples and CA to train and be certified as one of only 275 (I think) bona fide Neapolitans...it's thin crust, but not like a cracker; you get the doughy satisfaction. It's not Chicago deep-dish, or NY slice, or Seattle style, it's....different. And great.  See website for the particulars.

Don't be bashful about adding or deleting toppings from the menu items.  But...this place has so much more going for it.

Let's start with the oven-smoked bread...a hot, thick specimen served with herb butter and olive oil...one is plenty for two...

The salads use only fresh and snappy ingredients. Both house dressings are delicious and add a kick. The onion is unlike any other I've had in Tucson. The tomatoes are fleshy and tasty...all other appetizers  had the same hallmarks-

By all means, try the non-pizzas: the Calzone, filled with cheese and prosciutto, again plenty for two; the raviolis,  and any of the nightly specials posted on a blackboard outside or obtained from any of the friendly and caring staff-

You can have an intimate dinner; you can dine alone at single spots; and, you can have a group sampling all types of things.  The decor is unpretentious and stylish. You're made to feel comfortable and relaxed and not to go home hungry.

 (Alas, I have no knowledge of the desserts, but note raves from other yelpers.) This is not your father's/mother's "pizza place."

It's in a Basha's shopping plaza: easy in and out off Swan/Ft. Lowell. Plenty of parking within 20 yards.

My only regret is that I didn't have the rest of the family with me this trip to enjoy everything about Vero Amore.  But some of us will be coming back in April...see you there!

After a successful round of pho eating at Nam Phuong (review coming later because I'm in a backwards mood) the Pho fest embarked on the next destination. None of us felt quite ready to house a second bowl of pho (maybe at the Spring fest), but we were content with ordering banh mi to go and sampling some of their smoothies. and at the point of entry I hit the realization that I've been here before.

The first time I hit up Viet Huong was when I was a nearby resident who decided that he finally needed to hit up the Vietnamese Shopping Center  before moving away from the punk rock house of doom. When I entered I absolutely had NO IDEA where to go (before I learned that method of Yelp) and was thus easily intimidated by the sheer magnitude of options to try. So I stumbled in here and walked out with a dish of chicken fried rice that was ready to heat or eat self serve as was, and a rice triangle wrapped in seaweed. the chicken was nice, simple, and quite delectable. but the rice triangle radiated an aura that was rather fishy and not in the good way. but overall it was a solid place that had a lot more to try.... if my slacker mind didn't forget about it!

Foreshadow over to time number two after a successful edition of Record Club (bring it back Em Dish!). after filling our hearts content with good music Aaron suggested it for a parting dinner, and that ended up being my first attempt at trying congee. my unfamiliar ass would describe it as a Vietnamese porridge that somehow managed to have shrimp or another fish somewhat blended into not so successful secrecy. and it was a satisfactory and comforting experience I vowed to remember it so I could try it again, which didn't happen.

But the third time was the charm that had me try the fruitiest smoothie I ever did have, and I'm still not sure whether that makes the sweetness of the jack fruit a good thing or a bad. So freaking sweet! whether you go for pho (which will be done!) or congee, to take out Banh Mi or Vietnamese sausage, or to partake in drinks and their random karaoke room, Viet Huong leaves you with something interesting to take away. And you might have as hard a time figuring where the random clock like chime came from as I did (I still have no clue!). And now that I officially and finally have it stored in my brain, I can't wait to go back and take it all in once again. But I especially can't wait until tomorrow when I devour my 3rd banh mi of the week in Christopher R. like fashion!

I don't live in Mt. Airy and can say unequivocally that his place is worth the trip if you love good beer.  In particular, their Belgian Saison is really something special and as good as anything you'll find in Philadelphia.  The flatbreads are fine--I found the menu somewhat limited in its offerings, it's really just pizzas and salad and there certainly better places for both.  But the beer is the star of the show.

Paul B and Daniela B are rtght on the money-

Have been twice; weekdays both. Service excellent. Everything hot. We tried omelettes; we shared (three of us) this gigantic home-baked cinnamon roll as an appetizer; we had fresh cornbread to take home and savor.

Once I took friends who never eat bacon. They looked at the food one table over, ordered bacon, and wanted more-

Once I did the do it yourself omelette--different cheeses...delicious

If you are partial to grits, with hot melted butter, you must try theirs

But there are alternatives...fresh fruit, veggies to replace things like the hashed browns (cooked to order), the grits, the toast. Anyone on any breakfast schedule and have a real nice inexpensive breakfast out here.

In addition to the John Wayne pix, I saw alot of Seinfeld show photos, old Hollywood stuff, old Tucson...just the kind of ambience I like for breakfast. It's in a shopping plaza w ample parking. Enjoy!

There I was getting my first wax after birth with my new born baby on me! Mothers who need to get waxed - this is the place!

For those mother's of new born babies and those to come, this is the place for you. A month and a half after having given birth it was scary to even think about what was going on down there, especially since it had been a good 3-4 months since my last bikini wax - being a full 9 months preggers, the last think you are thinking about is getting waxed! 

So after a month and a half of having given birth I am ready for my first waxing. I packed up my kid in his infant car seat and headed to Salon International. Just as we were getting to my turn, my baby started crying. I figured that was it, I may as well pack up and go cause there is no way I would be able to let him cry while I get waxed and I was not leaving him. Emma stopped me and said, "it's ok, take the child, leave the car seat out front." That is exactly what I did.  She escorted me to her back room, told me to get in position on the waxing bed and lay my baby on my chest. The minute my baby and I laid down, my baby calmed down and enjoyed the scent of his mother. All the while I was also in a calm, relaxed state as I felt my baby's breathing on my chest.

I had dinner at Sampan a couple weekends ago. Overall, the experience was positive. I had an 8:30 pm reservation on a Saturday night. We ended up waiting an hour for our table because they were busy with a large party. Apparently several reservations were backed up. Although they made up for the wait by having the manager stop by at the end of the meal and taking off 20% of our bill, it would have been nice if they called to tell us that all their reservations were delayed for at least 45 minutes. The waiting period was brutal because the hostess kept saying "15 minutes. You're the next party on our list."

Waiting at their bar, however, wasn't too bad. The bartender was very attentive and worked efficiently. The atmosphere of the restaurant is trendy, but not over the top, complete with dim lighting for a slightly romantic feel and moderately loud music for a trendier weekend feel.

The service was pretty fast. I have no complaints about the food. It was all very good. We ordered their brussel sprouts, crispy noodles with crab, a hot appetizer that was similar to a dim sum dish and one of their fish items (I can't remember). We also ordered a dessert they had on special for Valentine's Day, which was a white chocolate mousse with a strawberry or raspberry sauce. It was so beautifully presented; just like one of those deconstructed desserts you might see on Food Network.

Overall, Sampan was an excellent restaurant that I would love to try again. I would recommend to anyone who is looking to go out for a special dinner but it is also good for large parties.

Such a good place to get quality food ! This is for sure a place you want to try if you live near or in Tampa.

Not so much if you are on a lunch break as it is very busy and not streamlined like downtown eateries but a good place when you have time.

I miss the day's when you could daily student prices.... (now only on thursdays). if you're a moviewatcher (free card) then you get the free popcorn on wednesdays as well. Either way it's nice that its within the mall so you can browse if you need to kill time. remember the 3rd floor of the parking lot takes you right to the outside entrance. No IMAX screen though- too bad.

I. LOVE. SEPHORA.  I'm like a kid in a candy store whenever I'm in there but the only problem is it's bad for my wallet.  Now, if that weren't an issue I'd spend as much time buying stuff there as I do going in and testing and browsing...Not only that, the staff is so friendly and knowledgeable.  One day, I needed disposable mascara wands because I was doing makeup for a play, so I called and asked if they sold them and they said, "No, we don't sell them but if you come by the store we'll leave some for you at the counter."  So I came in, and surely enough they gave me a whole bag full of cotton pads, eyeshadow and lipstick applicators, Q-Tips, and mascara wands!  There was a note on it too--"Dana--xoxo, Sephora."  I LOVE THESE PEOPLE!

The pizza here is ok, and the prices are pretty good, but two experiences have soured me on Top Tomato.

1.  One time we called out the guy at the register for using the same gloved hand to deal with money and food.

2.  One time we ordered fries and asked for a side of honey mustard at the register.  When they delivered the fries to our table (which we sent back because they were under cooked) they then tried to hit us up for an additional 50 cents for the honey mustard.  If they were going to tack on a mustard charge, you have to do it when we're paying not when the fries show up.

Seven of us ate here last night, and we all enjoyed our Thai food. Having been to Thailand, I am critical, and to me all the food here was reminiscent of food we had in Thailand, and very tasty. 

We had several curries, pad thai, and squid. The curries (green, yellow, red, and panang) all were appropriately different in spices, the squid was sweet and my wife said the pad thai was the best she has had. There were no chopped peanuts on it, however, but there was a generous helping of large fresh shrimp. Service was slow but good. Prices were very reasonable, nothing was over $10. They also have great $6.95 lunch specials.

They must have remodeled as we found the atmosphere and decor pleasant enough. The restaurant was about half full so it was comfortable and not overly noisy like many places.

This was my first time at Horizons. I loved it! I highly recommend it to anyone who wants to eat delicious gourmet food without worrying what has cream/butter/hidden meats. It was not cheap but worth it for a special occasion. The grilled seitan was so good! I'm still thinking about it. I didn't especially like the desserts they were just OK. But it won't stop me from going back. Also very nice ambiance and friendly service.

I decided to try Hummus today since I was coming to 40th street - PENN Dental School which is about a block away. I arrived slightly before the lunchtime crowd - the music that was playing was Bob Marley. "Oh goodie" I thought. "I feel like I am in the Meditteranean. I was hungry and thought I should have a substantial lunch because I may not eat until after the numbness has gone. So, I decided to try the Chicken Breast Platter which comes with two sides. I thought I should have Middle Eastern sides since this place is Middle Eastern. I chose the Baba Ganoush and Tabouli Salad. The platter also comes with Fries or Rice, Pita bread, which I suspect is homemade (but not Wheat) and Hummus and tahini. 

The Chicken was nice and tender, seasoned nicely and was delicious with or without the tahini sause. The Tabouli Salad was authentic and Baba ganoush flavorful, as was the Hummus - alittle too garlicky for my tastes. I also got the hand cut fries. They were fresh from  the fryer with their skins still on. 

Hummus has plenty of vegetarian options too.

it was a filling and tasty meal. It was nice hearing Reggae throughout lunch - XM Radio Reggae station...

I will come back again for lunch. It's economical, fairly authentic and pretty tasty.

Datz has been around for about a year or more now, and it seems to have hit on the right formula just recently by converting most of the downstairs deli into a cozy bar. Dim lighting, a large wall of craft beer taps and live music on certain nights combine to make this a cool hangout for the beautiful young types of South Tampa, never mind that most of them seem unjustifiably snobbish and snooty. Hey, they are 20-something and dumb. Whatever. The food is very good, but the portions are typically American: way too big for healthy eating. This means you are stuck lugging home a takeout container. I would cut the sandwich sizes and prices by at least a third. The homemade chips are good, slathered with some mystery sauce, which is probably not healthy either. The craft beers are the best thing about Datz. The service is spotty at best: most waiter are just young kids without a clue. One of them removed my pricey drink when it was still at least a third full. I told her about it and she poured me another, but still charged me. Duh! The owners here, I have heard, made a killing in some other business, so are spending a fortune running the place. I don't know if it's succeeding, but it is a nice alternative in S. Tampa. The parking war with Kalupa's Bakery next door is unfortunate. Evidently Kalupa's doesn't want the competition, so retaliates by having Datz customers towed, even at night when Kalupa's is closed. I have never eaten at Kalupa's, but I never will now after this absurd behavior.
Nice all around place for dinner, appetizers and/or drinks.

This chain is over hyped.   I had a slab of ribs that were tough, undercooked and nowhere near as good as they should have been.   I am used to fall off the bone ribs.  If that's what you are looking for, this is NOT the place.  Smells good, though.

IP, as the locals call it, opened right around 9/11 and faced concerns that customers might shy away. Since then, this somewhat upscale mall has survived four hurricanes in a row in 2004 and the 2008 economic meltdown. Stores have come and gone. Baystreet, the outdoor restaurant row behind the mall, has seen a bit of turnover as restaurants have gone out of business. The biggest restaurant space, which originally housed an awful place called Kahuna's, has remained empty and boarded up for years. Blue Martini, which attracts an Ed Hardy crowd, seems the most popular, but you can inexplicably stand in line for an hour or more to eat the oversized glop served by Cheesecake Factory. The best food is actually in the little cafe inside Nordstrom's, but few people seem to go there. This mall passes for upscale in a downscale town like Tampa, but it is far from top of the line. Orlando, about an hour east, has a true upscale mall called Millennia that shows how it should be done. Even so, I almost never buy anything at IP. It's very expensive and sales are not as frequent as some other malls, like the better-balanced Westshore Mall, just down the street. If you need designer clothes from Needless Markup (Neiman Marcus) or the latest duds from Diesel, you can find them here. People do like this mall. It is kept fairly clean, and the foodcourt is not the dirty, sticky circus you will find in some other malls. This one, though it features the same bad Chinese, rubbery pizza and generic sub sandwiches you can find  anywhere out on the street, at least looks semi-inviting, though the restrooms are not as clean as they should be. I enjoy an occasional stroll here to window shop and surf the latest gadgets at the always crowded (why?) Apple store. There are a lot of clothing stores for kids: AE, Hollister, etc, but at least they now have a Zara here for low-priced clothes with a little Euro style. This is not a bad place to kill an afternoon or early evening, and you may see NY Yankee shortstop and Tampa resident  Derrick Jeter having a Starbucks here. He often visits the mall to spend his bazillions.

Brushfire strikes me as a younger, "attitudinal," heavy metal BBQ place that is a bit different than what else is available in town.  I and my boy have enjoyed the pulled pork, smoked wings, and brisket.  There are a few differnet sauces that cover the spectrum from sweet to hot and in between, so most tastes should find something.  The sides are really hit or miss - the potato salad works for me.
The food is good and it is very reasonably priced. I really like the all you can eat chips, although the containers they give you are dinky.
LOVE AJ'S, I love getting a cup of coffee and sitting in the patio.

I have been coming to the Brew Pub a lot lately, usually because it's convenient to my house and has a comfy feel to it. Plus, everyone is nice there. The only thing I don't like about this place is that it always smells like beer (hence, the brew pub thing, but still, I don't remember all brew pubs always smelling like bad beer). But I digress. This place has consistently good food, not great food, but good food (though people I've brought here have always raved about it). And the unique factor alone is a sell. How many brew pubs have a sushi bar in the middle of them? But again, I digress. I'm mainly writing a review because of the pizzas. The Brew Pub's pizzas are AMAZING, better than most Italian joints I've frequented. Their claim to fame, at least in my opinion, is their new pizza that has panchetta and roasted corn on it. It comes out gooey and warm from the pizza oven. The crust is thin and crispy. And the taste is out of this world. It is a true guilty pleasure. I try so hard not to finish the whole pie but I ultimately do. And I don't even care. Go for the pizzas, you won't be sorry.

I went here based on a lot of the hype surrounding the place.  In some ways it didn't disappoint BUT in ways that I feel really mater it did!

What I really liked about the place was the fact that it was located on the 37th Floor and had great views of the city and surrounding areas.  You really did feel like you were in NYC as another reviewer posted. 
I loved how you checked the coat downstairs and took the private elevator to the restaurant.  We were immediately greated by a very pleasant hostess.  The bar is as others described, it was very hopping and for a Friday Happy Hour it was a great crowd. 

Our waiter came over to our table pretty quickly and he was nice BUT call it a pet peeve, he took our water order but never asked if we wanted a cocktail.  Okay...so it's FRIDAY and 6pm what's the first thing on your mind...get me a drink!  So he failed here!

We proceeded to order a 'snack' or appetizer which we asked to br brought out first, my partner ordered a salad and I ordered the soup and we each ordered our entree.  You probably guessed it already but just in case you didn't, they brought out our app and salad/soup at the same time.  They also brought the bread out at the same time as well...we felt like in 10 seconds were were bombarded from all sides!

The snack we ordered was the Rabbit Nachos...I thought they were great.  A little salty but great!  I never had rabbit before but after having this snack, I don't think I'd be afraid to order it again.

I got the onion soup and I loved the portion (not too much) but man was it super sweet...a little overwhelming actually.  I was hungry though so I did eat it all.  My partner ordered the caesar salad, I think, but he was unable to finish it though because it was way too salty.

Next I had the ribeye and when I say I make a more flavoful ribeye, I make a more flavorful ribeye.  It was a huge disappointment in my opinion.  My partner had the short ribs which were really good, they melted in your mouth.

So overall, I would totally go here for the bar especially for happy hour and probably order some apps.  For a dinner, I can think of a lot of other places to go that are twice as good for a 1/3 of the price.

If the pho ga does not cure your cold/flu, you might as well head for the emergency room because you are dying! The soups are THE BEST and especially great when you're sick. I haven't tried the sandwiches but those are next on my to-try list!

I really wanted to love this place, but I don't see what the hoopla is about. The day I went there, on a Saturday around 3 p.m., the food they served me was tasteless and OLD. You could tell the taco fillings were not fresh and had been sitting around for a while. Maybe they are cutting corners due to the economy.  I think it's probably a better idea to go to Chipotle; the food will be fresher because of the volume of their business.

I walked in a half-hour before they were supposed to close and they RUDELY shooed me out. ... You're a pizza place. It takes three minutes to reheat the pizza. ... Gimme a break. Terrible, terrible, terrible service. I mean, if you were not going to serve me, at least tell me CONGENIALLY that you are not serving any more.

I eat at this place many times while in Tucson.. It's consistenly good and interesting.  Last night I had a nice falafel and grill chicken on garden greens, hummus and pita, and their wonderful in house chicken soup. I've never had a  bad meal there.,  Give it a try, see what you think..

A friend and I went a couple of weeks ago so I could earn 1000 extra frequent flier miles as they were a participating restaurant in the promotion. 
Great service, fantastic food, terrific margaritas and some good beers on the menu.  
My only real complaint is that I don't quite get the concept.  It looks essentially the same as when Copa II was there except now they have tex-mex food instead of lots of burgers on the menu.  
don't get me wrong, the food was very good but I felt more like I was hanging out with one of the cool Mambo mover crews than stealing away to a mexican restaurnt.
Great place. Great coffee and great people. The food is pretty good too, tuna nicoise toasted is my fave.

Where oh where to begin.. This is probably one of the more recent reviews of Raw Lounge. I have been here twice and neither time left a wonderful rosy impression upon me. 

Food: I do have to admit that this is pretty decent sushi. The first time I went, my fiancee and I agreed that this was probably in our top 5 as far as quality. Osaka in Chestnut hill is better in our opinion. Raw's food isnt bad at all, but it wont be a transcendental experience by any means. 

Service: Downright horrible. This place is trying so hard to be trendy that they have ignored fundamental people and service skills. The first time we went, we were at least seated promptly, however the waitstaff was incredibly slow and essentially nonexistent. I was halfway considering canceling my order, paying for our drinks and leaving. But after our food arrived all was good until we attempted to get validation for parking in the lot next door. The hostess gave us a 20 minute run around back and forth between the parking lot and the restaurant before finally admitting that they were out of vouchers. Why didn't they tell us upfront? Is it necessary to deceive your customers that way? 

The second time we went, we didn't even eat. We were so angry that we left. We were a little late arriving for our reserved table so they gave it away. No big deal, I can understand that. We were told to wait at the bar for a table that would open up within the next 5 minutes. Quite some time went by and when I asked the hostess what the situation was with tables, I get a blank look, a mumbled answer and she walks away. At the bar, the bartender is very snotty with us even though we ordered drinks directly from him and tipped him. No fancy drinks that require extra prep, just beers. As we are being led to our table, he makes a very condescending remark to my fiancee. We bring this to the attention of the owner who says he will speak to the bartender, but the damage was already done. My fiancee was angry and hurt and we left. To be fair, the owner attempted to comp us for our beers however we had already paid. 

We had three people in our group. I was prepared to drop at least $150+ for dinner, but I didnt think the food would have mitigated the rudeness of the staff. I dont think I will be back anytime soon, nor will any of my friends and coworkers (Isn't it nice how quickly word gets around?) 

The decor is nice, the food is good, but the way the staff treats customers is simply horrible. Why would anyone put up with such egregious treatment from these people?

From the rustic bells hanging from the beautiful stainless-steel cart to the garlic surrounding the counters, to the music and singing and dancing of the chef -- you know you have stepped into a special microcosm divorced from its surroundings, then the food, warm pungent deep-green falafel, charred, perfectly-spiced chicken, amazingly fresh salad with large green grapes that look like they were picked from his own garden.
In return, you have to learn and accept the rules of this mad genius of food.  Pay attention to the patrons before you then go boldly to your turn.  Don't ask for Hummus unless you want to be banished to Mama's.

Watching the guy make crepes while sitting there eating mine was hypnotic. Plus it made me want to order other kinds to try them, it made me regret not being hungry for two, or three. ha. The selection of sweet and savory crepes was impressive with a dozen each, plus you could make your own. 

The European Crepe (w/o olives) was great with the salad like combination of greens, veggies, feta cheese, and dressing. The crepe just warmed the greens though to enhance their flavors, but not make them wilt. Warm greens and flavors were balanced well with neutral batter flavor. The price (7.50) was appropriate for the portion and it's quality.

I found this place based on one recommendation and all the previous yelp reviews. I went, had Emma, and was incredibly pleased. She is no nonsense but that's just philadelphia, and she is really good at waxing. I got a Brazilian the first time I was in, and while there were no frills (ie no gown to wear, no post-wax cream/oil) I was really pleased. Yeah, there was slight wax residue, but I used some baby oil to remove it, and took a shower that night. I've gone back twice since and started getting brows done by Emma. She really knows her stuff!!! I have had SO many bad brow experiences, and Emma made me feel like I could really trust her, and she did an excellent job.  With both the brazilian and brows, she tweezed ingrowns and missed areas. If you want to be pampered, don't come here, but if you want a fast, inexpensive and GOOD wax, you found your place.

I've had take-out margerita pizza here several times and it's good. Not the best but good. Obviously this is a coal-oven pizza joint but the crust for some reason tastes a little stale? It's not crispy on the outside warm/soft on the inside as you'd expect. It's pretty much rough all the way through.

Service isn't great. They wait staff barely talks to you, they mumble, & look totally miserable all while serving you. Also they overflowed my water glass at least 3 times leaving a wet mess for me to eat on. There weren't even that many people there at the time so there was no need to rush.

After stumbling upon reviews of QT, finally made the 2 block walk from my interior Chinatown dwelling to see what the hubbub was about. I tried a lemongrass tofu hoagie and took it back home to enjoy from the confines of my apartment as the restaurant itself is not much to look at, nor is the view out their front window, or maybe it was the fact that it only seats 6-7 comfortably. 

Regardless, I got home and unwrapped a perfectly made piece of heaven. By heaven I mean a sandwich made with just the right consistency of tofu balanced tastefully with fresh crunchy vegetables and loaded with cilantro on the best roll I have found in the neighborhood. I'm kind of like Joey from friends, I love a good sandwich, and this absolutely hit the spot. It was lemony without being overwhelming with a good amount of spice. And really, can you beat $4.95 for a non-overprocessed sandwich that is actually made with care?

My friend had discovered this place and wanted me to try it with him for lunch. So, today we ventured from our office and came here. It is a tiny, cramped space, no bigger than my Living Room with what I would describe as 'no frills' decor. Although the walls are painted a cheery orange. There is one table and two counters and we were lucky to get a spot! 

They have the much anticipated Vietnamese Hoagies: Every sandwich comes with fresh cilantro, pickled carrots,jalapeno peppers and cucumber on a fresh hoagie roll - hard on outside, soft and delicate on the inside. I had to try the Lemongrass Tofu Hoagie "Banh Mi Xa Chay". It was delicious. Filling, but, not too so. My friend had the Lemongrass Beef "Banh Mi Bo Xa". Hoagies $4.75 - 4.95.

if you don't want to try one of these delicious hoagies, they have Rice or Rice Noodle Platters, with the same igredients minus the hoagie roll. Platters $6.25 

Next time, I want to try the Tofu Sauteed with Onions and Mushrooms Hoagie! 

my advice: if you are going during the work week, go early since the restaurant is so tiny.

Also, FYI they do accept credit cards. My friend was able to use it his first time for his meal which was under $6.00.

This review is only based on getting drinks here.

the good - They have a very nice beer selection and casual ambience.

the bad - my first two beer requests were turned down, as they were out of stock.  This is unacceptable.  If you are out of something, make it extremely clear on the drink menu.  After I've agonized over several appealing drink choices, it is very bothersome to be told that the one I've finally settled on cannot be provided...twice.

I trust the reviews here enough to give it another shot, but I've got to say that my time here was definitely "meh. I've experienced better."

Get the gyoza soup. It's like wonton soup's prettier, smarter sister. You know the one. You see the family portrait and just feel bad for the ugly one. 

The soup is 'pricey' at 8.95 but it can serve a few as a side or be a meal for 2 people. Floating in a rich clear broth and surrounded by ribbons of cabbage and mushroom tasty plump gyoza that is packed with filling goodness. The broth is flavorful from the mushrooms and cabbage. The textures are all pretty consistent with soft mushrooms that just have a slight bite to them still, cabbage that is soft , but not mushy, and gyoza that is made of paper thin wonton liners. 

The sushi is fantastic too! I got a spicy tuna roll which was really good but the show stopper was the Don Roll. The inside is spice tuna but the outside is covered with thin pieces of eel, yellow belly tuna, and avocado. It's a huge roll and is a really good. The spicy tuna is actually spicy and the eel was cooked to perfection- slight tangy char on the edges. The yellow belly tuna is something new to me but it made a perfect menage a trois of fish.

I tried my friend's sweet potato roll and that was simple but good too. They just execute everything really well here.

Among other things, the C.T. Café puts out a fairly good and large pizza pie. They do come thin, which doesn't fit my dough loving nature, but the flavor is up there in quality. Price is a little high for the area which brings the rating down a star for me, as did thin crust, but to each their own. For the best local pizza in my eyes, hit Roma's on Ridge ave. (very doughy, but closed on Sunday and does not deliver). For those that crave the thin crust in the neighborhood, it doesn't get much better than Couch Tomato (only one comparable would be Alex's, but they are in a different league altogether).

**** Owner contacted me and assured me that he listens to all critiques on his business and strives to improve upon them, he even sent me a voucher for a free personal pan style pie to prove it. I have yet to redeem it, but hands on service and customer appreciation like that deserves a star increase in my book.

I am very particular about the make up I use. Once I find a product I love, it is nearly impossible for me to stray. However, the service at the Philadelphia Sephora location has caused me to rethink my policy. Each trip there is more dreadful than the last. My last trip was especially painful. I went in to purchase a replacement product which was out of stock and spent the next 45 minutes being shuffled between employees who would rather watch paint dry than answer my questions. I was never even told the item was out of stock, I was just thrown on the phone with customer service who had no idea what was going on either. If asking a simple question (when will you be getting this product back in?) is so difficult to answer, from now I will just make everyone's life easier and shop at MAC.

Stopped here for lunch today and will definitely have to go back. A local upscale sandwich ship with some pasta's /  great gelatto. it's a must try place. Flatbread pizza looked great though I didn't have it myself. I agree with Bill B the olives were a but on the over salted side.

I've never had chaat before and the owner was too happy to describe the various spices used for the chaats. I decided to try the Samosa Chaat, because I love Samosas! the owner also said back in his homeland, Pakistan, the women would have chaat before shopping, which makes sense. So, it's more of a snack in Pakistan,but, here in Philly, it was our lunch!

It was very satisfying - the spices, lentils and chickpeas and bits of mango on top to refresh the palatte. 

I want to come back with my friend to try a different chaat. In fact, we want to try them all!
